claims.evidence_text: Jamieson et al. (1998b) calculated HS in relation to Tt accumulated above a base temperature of 0 °C assuming it takes one phyllochron for emergence to occur.
claims.claim_summary: Thermal time for wheat leaf appearance and development is accumulated above a base temperature of 0 °C.

claims.evidence_text: The base phyllochron was increased to 120 °Cd, emergence took 0·9 phyllochron, and the break points for change in phyllochron were positioned at 2·5 and 7·0 HS.
claims.claim_summary: Base phyllochron (thermal-time interval between successive leaf appearances) for 'Batten' wheat was 120 °Cd per leaf.

claims.evidence_text: The appearance of leaves on the main stem is a linear function of temperature ( Kirby, 1995 ) from close to 0 °C to at least 30 °C ( Jamieson et al. , 2008 ), but may peak and then reduce with increasing temperatures above 30 °C.
claims.claim_summary: Wheat main-stem leaf appearance increases linearly with temperature from near 0 °C to at least 30 °C, peaking and declining above 30 °C.

claims.evidence_text: The value of –1·5 was calculated from the Tt from sowing to emergence (110 °Cd) and assuming ‘phyllochron’ was 60 % of the base phyllochron (120 °Cd) for negative HS.
claims.claim_summary: Thermal time from sowing to emergence in 'Batten' wheat was 110 °Cd (above 0 °C base).

claims.evidence_text: Vt accumulates at 1 unit per day between 4 and 10 °C, but reduces linearly to zero at temperatures above 16 °C and below –4 °C in ARCWHEAT1.
claims.claim_summary: Vernalization time accumulates at 1 unit/day between 4 and 10 °C, declining to zero above 16 °C and below −4 °C (ARCWHEAT1).

claims.evidence_text: The temperature that facilitates the fastest reduction in FLN is 8 °C and the rate decreases to have no effect at 0 and 15 °C.
claims.claim_summary: In SIRIUS, the vernalization temperature giving the fastest reduction in final leaf number for winter wheat is 8 °C, with no effect at 0 and 15 °C.

claims.evidence_text: a number of datasets show a lag period of up to 45 d exposure to cold conditions before a reduction in FLN occurs ( Wang et al. , 1995 a , b ; Brooking, 1996 ; Brooking and Jamieson, 2002 ).
claims.claim_summary: Winter wheat can show a lag of up to 45 days of cold exposure before vernalization reduces final leaf number.

claims.evidence_text: Shorter Pp facilitate an increase in the number of leaves that are added to FLN and this increases linearly from 0 at Pp above 16 h to a maximum at Pp below 8 h.
claims.claim_summary: Wheat photoperiod response saturates outside 8–16 h: leaves added to final leaf number increase linearly from zero above 16 h to a maximum below 8 h.

claims.evidence_text: short days may accelerate development during vernalization ... but delay development following vernalization ... apparent rates of Vrn3 expression increased from 0·15/HS at 8-h Pp to 0·33/HS at 16-h Pp.
claims.claim_summary: Wheat shows opposite photoperiod responses before and after vernalization saturation; post-VS, long days (16 h vs 8 h) accelerate reproductive (Vrn3) progress and reduce final leaf number.
